---
title: "Lobster Report"
author: Denis and Angie
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Introduction

In this report, we aim to better understand how lobster populations and fishing activity change over time, which is essential for managing marine ecosystems. Here we specifically analyze patterns in lobster abundance, size, and fishing pressure across multiple reef sites in the Santa Barbara Channel.

## Data Source

The data in this report come from the Environmental Data Initiative data package:

-   Santa Barbara Coastal LTER, D. Reed, and R. Miller. 2022. SBC LTER: Reef: Abundance, size and fishing effort for California Spiny Lobster (Panulirus interruptus), ongoing since 2012 ver 8. Environmental Data Initiative. <https://doi.org/10.6073/pasta/25aa371650a671bafad64dd25a39ee18.> Accessed April 16, 2026.

This data package contains two tables, one focused on lobster abundance and size, and one focused on lobster trap buoy counts. `Lobster_Trap_Counts_All_Years_20210519.csv`and `Lobster_Abundance_All_Years_20220829.csv`

## Abstract Summary

This dataset examines California spiny lobster ,*Panulirus interruptus*, abundance, size, and fishing pressure along the mainland coast of the Santa Barbara Channel. Lobsters are important predators in giant kelp forests, this data sets helps researchers study how fishing and marine protected areas may influence kelp forest communities.

## Owner Analysis and Visualization

### Lobster Size Distribution by Site

![Histogram showing lobster size distribution across sites](figs/lobster_histogram_owner.png)

The size distributions in these histograms show clear differences in population structure across sites. With IVEE and CARP having larger average sizes and wider distributions, which suggests healthier or more mature lobster populations at these locations. In contrast, AQUE and MOHK are more tightly clustered around smaller sizes, indicating either younger populations or possible environmental or fishing pressures limiting growth. NAPL is somewhat in between, with a moderate spread that suggests a mix of size classes but fewer very large individuals.

### Lobster Abundance Over Time

![Line graph showing lobster counts by year and site](figs/lobster_line_plot.png)

This lobster abundance plot shows trends that vary significantly by site, with IVEE showing the highest peaks and the most pronounced fluctuations over time. CARP also exhibits strong variability, including a notable spike around 2017, suggesting periods of increased recruitment or favorable conditions. In contrast, AQUE remains consistently low and relatively stable, indicating limited population growth or persistent constraints. MOHK and NAPL show moderate but changing trends, suggesting that both environmental variability and fishing pressure may influence lobster abundance at these sites.

## Collaborator Analysis and Visualization

Data :Lobster_Trap_Counts_All_Years_20210519.csv\
In this part of our analysis we worked on the lobster trap counts data. Focusing on two questions, how total trap counts changed over time at each site, and how more recent observations from 2019 to 2021 can be categorized into high and low fishing pressure.

![Line plot showing total lobster trap buoy counts over time across sites.](figs/lobster_line_and_point_plot.png)

This plot shows how total trap buoy counts changed over time at each site. Some sites show higher overall trap counts others remain comparatively low. Here we can see the differences in fishing activity across locations.

![Grouped bar chart showing high and low fishing pressure observations at each site from 2019 to 2021.](figs/lobster_pressure_plot.png)

This plot compares the number of high and low fishing pressure observations across sites for the years 2019 to 2021. ( \< 8 = low/ \>= 8 = high ) Some sites have more frequent high trap counts, which indictates greater fishing pressure during that time.

## Discussion

Across all analyses, clear site-specific patterns emerge in lobster size, abundance, and fishing pressure. Sites like IVEE and CARP not only show larger and more widely distributed lobster sizes but also exhibit higher and more variable abundance over time, suggesting more established or productive populations. In contrast, AQUE and MOHK tend to have smaller, more concentrated size distributions and lower or more stable abundance, which may reflect environmental limitations or greater external pressures.

The trap count data adds an important layer by showing that fishing pressure is uneven across sites, with some locations experiencing consistently higher levels of activity, particularly in recent years. These differences in fishing intensity may help explain variations in both lobster size and abundance, as higher pressure can limit growth and reduce population size. Overall, the combined patterns suggest that lobster populations are shaped by an interaction of environmental conditions and human impacts, with potential influences from site protection status such as marine protected areas.
